message.content: find the procedure in this project relating to disabling microsoft sleep

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
The Windows sleep/hibernate disable procedure lives in the **Caparison Windows** spec.

**Primary site — `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc:285`** (Phase 3):
- `powercfg /change standby-timeout-ac 0`
- `powercfg /change standby-timeout-dc 0`
- `powercfg /hibernate off`
- Run over key-only ssh, idempotent

**Related sites:**
- `BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows.adoc:137-160` — the verification side: asserts `STANDBYIDLE` index is `0x00000000` via `powercfg /query SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_SLEEP STANDBYIDLE`, and asserts hibernate absent via `powercfg /a`.
- `BUSJHW-HandbookWindows.adoc:109` — handbook prose noting caparison applies `powercfg /change standby-timeout-ac 0`.
- `BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc:1763, 1826, 2002` — overview references in the umbrella spec.

Links: [{"title":"Enable Hibernate on Windows 11 from Command Prompt, PowerShell, and Registry","url":"https://pureinfotech.com/enable-hibernate-windows-11/"},{"title":"How to Use PowerShell to Enable or Disable Hibernation on Windows","url":"https://www.groovypost.com/howto/use-powershell-to-enable-or-disable-hibernation-on-windows/"},{"title":"How to see from commandline (!!!) if hibernation is on or off? - Windows 7 Help Forums","url":"https://www.sevenforums.com/installation-setup/390268-how-see-commandline-if-hibernation-off.html"},{"title":"Win10 Hibernation Situation - Windows Enterprise Desktop","url":"https://www.techtarget.com/searchenterprisedesktop/blog/Windows-Enterprise-Desktop/Win10-Hibernation-Situation"},{"title":"How to disable and re-enable hibernation - Windows Client | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/setup-upgrade-and-drivers/disable-and-re-enable-hibernation"},{"title":"How to Enable Hibernation on Windows 11","url":"https://www.howtogeek.com/how-to-enable-hibernation-on-windows-11/"},{"title":"Enable or Disable Hibernate in Windows 10 - Windows 10 Help Forums","url":"https://www.tenforums.com/tutorials/2859-enable-disable-hibernate-windows-10-a-8.html"},{"title":"Solved: Powershell to query current hibernate status of all Windows Server in the domain ? | Experts Exchange","url":"https://www.experts-exchange.com/questions/26942015/Powershell-to-query-current-hibernate-status-of-all-Windows-Server-in-the-domain.html"},{"title":"How to Disable or Enable Hibernate in Windows 10 & 11 - MajorGeeks","url":"https://www.majorgeeks.com/content/page/how_to_disable_or_enable_hibernate_in_windows_10.html"},{"title":"How to Enable Or Disable Hibernate Mode In Windows 10/11","url":"https://www.techbout.com/enable-disable-hibernate-mode-windows-10-45758/"}]

I've performed a web search for "hibernation enabled status PowerShell Windows" and found several relevant resources. Here's what I found:

## Checking Hibernation Status

To check the feature's current state, you can run a command in PowerShell. By running a verification command, the first section will tell you hibernation's current status.

## Managing Hibernation with PowerShell

To turn hibernation off, enter the command `powercfg /hibernation off` in an administrative Command (cmd.exe) or PowerShell session. A similar string turns hibernation back on: `powercfg /hibernation on`

To enable or disable hibernation with PowerShell, open PowerShell as admin and use the commands: `powercfg -h off` to disable or `powercfg -h on` to enable.

## Additional Information

The Hiberfil.sys hidden system file is located in the root folder of the drive where the operating system is installed, with a size approximately equal to the amount of RAM installed. The computer uses this file to store a copy of system memory on the hard disk when the hybrid sleep setting is turned on.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.query: powercfg /a output parse hibernation available PowerShell check

content.content:
Web search results for query: "powercfg /a output parse hibernation available PowerShell check"

Links: [{"title":"Powercfg command-line options | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/design/device-experiences/powercfg-command-line-options"},{"title":"How to Use PowerShell to Enable or Disable Hibernation on Windows","url":"https://www.groovypost.com/howto/use-powershell-to-enable-or-disable-hibernation-on-windows/"},{"title":"Enable Hibernate on Windows 11 from Command Prompt, PowerShell, and Registry","url":"https://pureinfotech.com/enable-hibernate-windows-11/"},{"title":"Enable or Disable Hibernate in Windows 10 - Windows 10 Help Forums","url":"https://www.tenforums.com/tutorials/2859-enable-disable-hibernate-windows-10-a-8.html"},{"title":"How to remove Sleep (and disable it) and add Hibernate to the Windows 11 Start menu power options using Powershell? | Windows 11 Forum","url":"https://www.elevenforum.com/t/how-to-remove-sleep-and-disable-it-and-add-hibernate-to-the-windows-11-start-menu-power-options-using-powershell.8992/"},{"title":"How to Disable or Enable Hibernate in Windows 10 & 11 - MajorGeeks","url":"https://www.majorgeeks.com/content/page/how_to_disable_or_enable_hibernate_in_windows_10.html"},{"title":"How to Enable or Disable Hibernation using PowerCFG command line in Windows 11/10","url":"https://www.wsxdn.com/system/windows/114314.html"},{"title":"How to see from commandline (!!!) if hibernation is on or off? - Windows 7 Help Forums","url":"https://www.sevenforums.com/installation-setup/390268-how-see-commandline-if-hibernation-off.html"},{"title":"Enable or Disable Hibernation using PowerCFG command ...","url":"https://www.thewindowsclub.com/enable-disable-hibernation-using-powercfg"},{"title":"How to disable and re-enable hibernation - Windows Client | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/setup-upgrade-and-drivers/disable-and-re-enable-hibernation"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about checking hibernation availability using the `powercfg` command:

## Using `powercfg /a` to Check Hibernation

You can use the command `powercfg –availablesleepstates` (or `powercfg /a`) to check the available sleep states from the command line.

## PowerShell Method to Check Hibernation Status

For a more programmatic approach in PowerShell, you can use this PowerShell script to check hibernation status:

```powershell
$hibernation = Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_PowerManagementCapabilities | Select-Object HiberFilePresent
if ($hibernation.HiberFilePresent -eq $true) { 
    Write-Host "Hibernation is on" 
} else { 
    Write-Host "Hibernation is off" 
}
```

## Enabling/Disabling Hibernation

To enable hibernation on Windows 11, you can run the "powercfg /hibernate on" command, and to disable hibernation you can use `powercfg -h off`.

The `powercfg /a` command will display all available sleep states on your system, which will indicate whether hibernation is available and supported on your hardware.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about checking hibernation availability using the `powercfg` command:

## Using `powercfg /a` to Check Hibernation

You can use the command `powercfg –availablesleepstates` (or `powercfg /a`) to check the available sleep states from the command line.

## PowerShell Method to Check Hibernation Status

For a more programmatic approach in PowerShell, you can use this PowerShell script to check hibernation status:

```powershell
$hibernation = Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_PowerManagementCapabilities | Select-Object HiberFilePresent
if ($hibernation.HiberFilePresent -eq $true) { 
    Write-Host "Hibernation is on" 
} else { 
    Write-Host "Hibernation is off" 
}
```

## Enabling/Disabling Hibernation

To enable hibernation on Windows 11, you can run the "powercfg /hibernate on" command, and to disable hibernation you can use `powercfg -h off`.

The `powercfg /a` command will display all available sleep states on your system, which will indicate whether hibernation is available and supported on your hardware.

content.text:
Here's how to query hibernation status via PowerShell — three idioms, in order of project-fit:

**1. `powercfg /a` (parse stdout)** — what the project already uses in `BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows.adoc:151-160`. Run `powercfg /a` and check whether "Hibernate" appears under the "available on this system" list. Absent ⇒ disabled.

**2. `powercfg /a | Select-String Hibernate`** — PowerShell-native filter on the same command:
```powershell
if ((powercfg /a | Out-String) -match 'Hibernate(?!.*not available)') { "on" } else { "off" }
```
More fragile (depends on negative-lookahead against the section split); the existing approach of asserting absence from the available-states section is cleaner.

**3. CIM/WMI** — programmatic, no text parsing:
```powershell
(Get-CimInstance Win32_PowerManagementCapabilities).HiberFilePresent
```
Returns `$true`/`$false`. Caveat: reflects `hiberfil.sys` presence, not whether the user-facing hibernate menu entry is wired up. Some sources note this can diverge from `powercfg /a`'s view on machines where the file exists but hibernation is policy-disabled.

For matching the existing Invigilate Windows assertion pattern (`«POWERCFG_REPORT»` capture, then check Hibernate absent from the available-states block), staying on `powercfg /a` is the most coherent choice — it's already the contract on the caparison side.

Sources:
- [Powercfg command-line options | Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/design/device-experiences/powercfg-command-line-options)
- [How to disable and re-enable hibernation | Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/setup-upgrade-and-drivers/disable-and-re-enable-hibernation)
- [How to Use PowerShell to Enable or Disable Hibernation on Windows](https://www.groovypost.com/howto/use-powershell-to-enable-or-disable-hibernation-on-windows/)
- [Enable or Disable Hibernation using PowerCFG command line](https://www.thewindowsclub.com/enable-disable-hibernation-using-powercfg)
- [How to see from commandline if hibernation is on or off?](https://www.sevenforums.com/installation-setup/390268-how-see-commandline-if-hibernation-off.html)

lastPrompt: websearch how to query whether hibernation is enabled via powershell.
